policy_statement: A13: Adapt the transport system to extreme weather by incorporating climate considerations into design standards, materials and procurement; consider permeable pavements, tree planting/grassing of tracks, and develop emergency scenarios for service disruptions (continuous).
mechanism_description: It incorporates climate considerations into transport design and procurement and elaborates emergency scenarios to reduce extreme-weather-related infrastructure damage and service disruptions.
primary_causal_pathway: Climate hazard → ↓ infrastructure vulnerability/exposure + ↑ preparedness → ↓ damage & ↓ transport service disruption
causal_mechanism_detail: (1) Change: Transport assets and operations are modified via climate-resilient design/procurement (e.g., heat- and water-tolerant materials, permeable pavements, vegetated/treated trackbeds) and formal emergency/continuity planning for disruptive events. (2) Mediating process: Engineering and operational resilience reduces physical failure (washouts, overheating/buckling, drainage exceedance), speeds recovery through pre-planned procedures and redundancy, and reduces cascading network failures during extremes. (3) Outcome: Lower climate-impact losses (asset damage, downtime, safety incidents) and improved continuity of mobility and access during and after extreme weather.
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is reduced extreme-weather-related damage and service interruptions in transport systems by lowering exposure/sensitivity and improving response capacity.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: climate hazards threaten transport assets/operations; resilient design and emergency planning reduce vulnerability and improve response, cutting disruption and damage—an adaptation pathway.  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: while some measures (e.g., permeable pavements, tree planting) can have minor mitigation co-benefits (urban cooling, modest sequestration, potential energy savings), the dominant and intended effect across policies using this mechanism is maintaining service and reducing climate-impact losses, not reducing emissions.  
3) Edge cases applied: nature-based elements (tree planting/grassing) are treated here as enabling infrastructure resilience (cooling, drainage) rather than primarily enhancing carbon sinks; any mitigation outcomes would be secondary.  
4) Applicability to ALL instances: any policy fitting this mechanism—climate-resilient transport design/procurement plus emergency planning—fundamentally targets hazard-induced disruption risk reduction, which is categorically adaptation.  
5) Secondary subtype assignment (e.g., flood resilience vs heat resilience; grey vs green infrastructure; preparedness vs hardening) is deferred to policy-level evidence.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: If the same intervention is framed/implemented primarily to reduce operational energy use or GHG emissions (e.g., materials chosen mainly for embodied-carbon reduction), that would be a different mechanism; here the defining feature is hazard resilience and continuity planning. Nature-based additions (trees/grass) may also fit Nature-Based Solutions in other mechanisms if carbon sink or ecosystem enhancement is primary, but in this mechanism they function mainly as resilience measures.
key_indicators: “designed with consideration to the prognostications”; “enforced in the planning process”; “public procurement process”; “elaborate an emergency scenario”; “operating disruptions caused by extreme weather conditions”; “extreme weather phenomena”
co_benefits: Improved day-to-day asset reliability and safety; potential urban greening/amenity and air-quality benefits if tree planting/grassing is implemented.
instance_edge_case_notes: Procurement is mentioned (“public procurement process”), which could support classifying as a procurement-requirement, but the core instrument is broader integration/enforcement through planning/design processes plus emergency scenario development, so planning-strategy is the best single fit.
